replies.author: @Aidar77
replies.text:
I first read the books back in the ’90s and was struck by the sheer depth and craftsmanship of Sapkowski’s world. This was never a local oddity as some people here claim— the saga has been translated into dozens of languages before the games. Now the list is 37 and counting, and it was never ‘popular only in Poland’ as some "experts" here state. Neither was is popular "only because of games". It was bought by CD Project Red and made into sequel, only because they knew that books had a solid fan base all over the world, who were waiting for a franchise to be brought back to life. 

It was a narrative goldmine waiting to be adapted, yet somehow it was squandered. The degree of mismanagement needed to derail material this rich is staggering.
And to anyone who insists the books are ‘just’ Ciri’s coming-of-age story: read them. The novels are dense with political manoeuvring, legal and ideological conflict, and statecraft worthy of comparison to other great political fantasies — far more than a single-arc bildungsroman. 

If you reduce Sapkowski to a one-note plot, you’re not arguing — you’re projecting laziness or ignorance. Don’t pretend familiarity when all you’ve done is skim a Netflix summary.

@Volkbrecht Yeah, this particular part is the one thing that actually arguably is closer to the actual source material than the games were. The games (well, the third one anyway, which is the only one most people know) very much made Geralt not just the POV character but also the protagonist, whereas in the books, it's much more nuanced. He is the vessel through which the reader sees the world, but he's often not the major mover of the plot or the most important character.

Don't get me wrong, the TV series sucks. But if one wants to talk about "respecting the source material," ranting about Geralt playing a lesser role is one of the worst ways to try to make that point.

Can we stop the crap? You clearly know NOTHING about Tolkien. Actually the drinker liking this comment makes me unfollow. Peter Jackson never cared about Tolkien, the lore or anything. He just knew how to make movies that are appealing to the mass. He actually centered his movies around things that are not important and hardly or not mentioned in the books and brought in all the hollywood tribes Tolkien loathed and fought against all his life. You can google what his son and heir said about the movies and if his father would have liked them. 
Most things that are really important in the books never made it into the movie like the love between Frodo and Sam which is deep and true and never wavers. Yes, love and absolute trust. Frodo is not young but very intelligent and wise and he never trusts Gollum. He can understand a few things about him and he pities him. There is no choice to make between him and Sam, because Gollum is never even a friend or liked by any of them. 
Aragorn also never chooses between Arwen and Eowyn. The idea is ludicrous because Eowyn is like a child to Aragorn. He has devoted his life to prove that he is worthy to marry Arwen and he already spend 40 years doing that. The ring quest is part of this. But everything he does and he becomes is for Arwen. She is not a girl looking like a Supermodel and riding against Ring Wraiths alone. Her position is unique to all elves and her power is not a physic one. She is the greatest treasure the elven race still possess and they know they will lose her forever if Sauron is defeated and that eventually happens.

replies.text: To be honest, his only expression in the show is irritation. In the books Gerald was way more expressive, rambling about philosophy, politics and constantly making sarcasm. But instead Cavil played someone who would show up on a search page after typing in “brutal stoic”.

replies.author: @maggiem6209
replies.text:
​@pesticide1596 True, he could have made Geralt more contemplative and more chatty. And I would have really liked to see him banter with Dandelion. But I remember in an interview that he gave he specifically said that he chose to play Geralt that way because he felt that too much dialogue wouldn't allow him to communicate Geralt as a person in 1 hour long episodes, and due to the way the franchise was adapted, it drew off of the few parts of the books they used and the game portrayal. Too much chattering may have made him more akin to Dandelion in an unhelpful way or given the writers too much opportunity to shoehorn bad lines. 

I'm not an actor, but I think he did a great job with what he had to work with. I would have liked to see more of his book personality, but I also get that that may not have worked. I understand the frustration, though. Very reasonable criticism.

author: @R_Adam_K
text: Brother, I’m Polish — born in ’89. I still remember riding the train with my Walkman, listening to The Witcher audiobooks every day and going to bed dreaming about how amazing a movie adaptation could be. You can’t imagine how crushing it is to see what we ended up with. Henry was great, but the soul of Sapkowski’s world is just gone. On the other hand, I read LOTR before the movies came out, and I was stunned at how close Peter Jackson got to what I imagined. That’s what The Witcher deserves — a Peter Jackson-level adaptation.

author: @herbatnik_
text:
When it comes to Andrzej Sapkowski's involvement in the show production, it's important to know the context. When the production of first season was in a full swing, Andrzej was mourning loss of his son who died in the mid of 2019. The same son who told his father to send his stories to "Fantastyka" (polish fantasy magazine) and that's how Witcher started.
While of course Andrzej's personality and "business" sense are - let's say - not the best possible that one could have (he very much despised games whole time), I could imagine that he just didn't care as much and didn't have will to spend time with some randoms fighting over their changes in the lore. Maybe he was just in "I'm tired, give me money and get out" state

How it went we all know, but he was an old grumpy man who didn't understand technology even then, so he made an understandable choice. author: @Ryslaw
text:
I read the first short story back in January of 1987. I became a fan, immediately. I bought every issue of Fantastyka magazine hoping to find Andrzej Sapkowski's  stories. Then, in the 90s I  bought the short stories collection in the book form, then the original Polish comics, and of course THE SAGA. And then there was the Polish tv show, that we all made fun of at that time (silly us, especially considering the Netflix abomination). And then I  became the project lead for the first Witcher game. 

And I absolutely detest the netflix show. Before it started, I had had some hopes. They were shattered in the first episode of the first season. Pathetic, worthless, shitty drivel with Henry Cavill as the only saving grace. Now, without him, it looks like something so horribly bad that I wouldn’t touch it with a ten-foot pole. (Pole-pun intended!)

It is such a pity that they butchered these wonderful stories. Fortunately, the new Witcher novel by Andrzej Sapkowski is very good. I hope you will be able to read it English soon, although I feel sorry for the translator, because AS has laid countless linguistic traps and embellished his language even further.

And greetings to all true Witcher fans!

text: Netflix was a big loser in this, too, by entrusting the source material to writers who not only didn't respect the source material and the star, they were mediocre at best. author: @HeyCousin
text:
Imagine yourself being a showrunner with good intentions, out to make the most accurate, faithful and likable fantasy show based on a book series you love. Then imagine someone like Cavill is your lead actor and you get direct access to the original author, what a dream come true that would be (albeit Sapkowski is a little strange when it comes to gatekeeping). You'd get along like a house on fire with someone who loves the source material as much as you do and you know they're going to give the part their all, just like you will. The end product is well liked, praised for it's lore accuracy, makes your studio millions and in future regarded as one of the best fantasy shows ever produced.

This is what would have happened if ANY fan of the Witcher was hired as the showrunner for this, no matter their experience it would have, without a doubt, turned out better than the reality, but alas the show was made in the modern day by petty, vindictive and ultimately empty vessels of THE MESSAGE. RIP Netflix's Witcher, you will not be missed.
